original_abstract: This article examines the contemporary linguistic landscape of Cieszyn and Český Těšín, sibling cities divided by the Olza River since 1920. Following a brief historical overview of Těšín Silesia’s multi-ethnic heritage and an outline of current Czech language policies regarding the Polish minority in the Zaolzie region, the author investigates how bilingualism manifests in public spaces today. The study analyzes authentic linguistic material to explore the nuances of both official and informal communication on both sides of the border. Specifically, the author evaluates the quality of written and spoken language through the lens of lexical borrowings, diminutive usage, interlingual homonymy (false friends), and graphical elements such as orthography and font styles. By situating these texts within their cultural and pragmatic contexts, the article identifies the intended recipients of Polish signage in Czechia and Czech advertising in Poland. Ultimately, the research reveals how the visual and communicative environment of these twin cities reflects broader differences in national minority rights, commercial interests, and the unique cross-border identity of the region’s inhabitants.
humanized_abstract: Abstract: Since 1920 the Těšín Silesia towns of Cieszyn and Český Těšín, the so-called sister cities, have divided the Olza river. This article touches on the multicultural history of Těšín Silesia, presents an overview of Czech language policy towards Polish national minorities in Zaolzie and subsequently focuses on the current state of bilingualism in both towns on either side of the border, with the aim of providing an analysis of the linguistic representation of minority communities on a communicative plan at the border. Special attention is paid to the assessment of written and verbal bilingual expressions represented by examples of lexical contact, (linguistic) diminutives, interlinguval homonymy or orthographic and graphical aspects of the used fonts. The entire linguistic material will be thematically incorporated into the specific cultural and communicative context, and the target audiences of Polish labels located in Czech republic as well as of Czech advertisements in Poland will be determined. Final conclusion drawn in this article is that current visual communication in Cieszyn and Český Těšín reflexes discrepancies existing in protection of national minorities in individual countries, as well as the different strategies of respective economies (commerce) that unequivocally stress and strengthen the specific transborder identity of border inhabitants.
